title: Quiz: Symmetric Cryptography
description: Test your understanding of symmetric encryption, AES, and block cipher modes

questions.question: What is the primary characteristic that distinguishes symmetric encryption from asymmetric encryption?

options.text: Symmetric encryption is faster
Symmetric encryption uses the same key for encryption and decryption
Symmetric encryption is more secure
Symmetric encryption doesn't require keys

questions.explanation: The defining characteristic of symmetric encryption is that the same secret key is used for both encryption and decryption. While symmetric encryption is indeed faster, this is a consequence, not the defining characteristic.

questions.question: Why should ECB (Electronic Codebook) mode NEVER be used in practice?

options.text: It's too slow for practical applications
It requires too much memory
Identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, leaking patterns
It doesn't support key sizes larger than 128 bits

questions.explanation: ECB mode encrypts each block independently with the same key, so identical plaintext blocks always produce identical ciphertext blocks. This preserves patterns in the data (famously demonstrated by the 'ECB penguin' image).

questions.question: What block size does AES use?

options.text: 64 bits
128 bits
256 bits
Variable (depends on key size)

questions.explanation: AES always uses a 128-bit (16-byte) block size, regardless of key size. The key size (128, 192, or 256 bits) affects the number of rounds but not the block size.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of GCM mode over CBC mode?

options.text: GCM is faster
GCM uses smaller keys
GCM provides authentication (integrity) in addition to encryption
GCM doesn't require an IV

questions.explanation: GCM (Galois/Counter Mode) is an authenticated encryption mode that provides both confidentiality AND integrity. CBC only provides confidentiality—an attacker could modify the ciphertext without detection.

questions.question: How does Grover's algorithm affect symmetric encryption security?

options.text: It completely breaks all symmetric encryption
It has no effect on symmetric encryption
It effectively halves the key length (256-bit becomes 128-bit security)
It only affects encryption but not decryption

questions.explanation: Grover's algorithm provides a quadratic speedup for searching, reducing an n-bit key's security to n/2 bits. AES-256 with 256-bit key provides 128-bit security against quantum attacks, which is still considered secure.

questions.question: ChaCha20-Poly1305 is faster than AES-GCM in software on devices without hardware AES acceleration.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. ChaCha20 was specifically designed to be fast in software. On devices without AES-NI (hardware AES acceleration), ChaCha20-Poly1305 significantly outperforms AES-GCM.

questions.question: What happens if you reuse a nonce with the same key in AES-GCM?

options.text: Nothing, GCM handles nonce reuse gracefully
Encryption becomes slower
Both confidentiality and authenticity are completely broken
Only the authentication tag becomes invalid

questions.explanation: Nonce reuse with GCM is catastrophic. If the same nonce is used twice with the same key, an attacker can recover the authentication key (breaking integrity) and XOR plaintexts together (breaking confidentiality). Never reuse nonces!

questions.question: Which of the following are TRUE about stream ciphers? (Select all that apply)

options.text: They encrypt data one bit or byte at a time
They generate a pseudorandom keystream from the key
They require padding to handle arbitrary-length messages
ChaCha20 is an example of a stream cipher

questions.explanation: Stream ciphers encrypt data one bit/byte at a time by XORing with a keystream generated from the key. ChaCha20 is a stream cipher. Unlike block ciphers, stream ciphers do NOT require padding since they can encrypt any length of data.
